critique.summary: The article presents a study on behavioral convergence between two advanced language models, GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6, showing that both models produce deterministic empty outputs when prompted with ontologically null concepts under embodiment prompting. The study claims reproducible cross-model results, demonstrating a shared boundary in language model behavior that prevents continuation on certain semantic voids.
mainPoints: GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6 both return deterministic empty outputs for core null prompts under embodiment prompting.
The behavior is reproducible across models and independent of token budget.
The models show partial resistance to adversarial attempts to bypass this silence.
Explicit permission to remain silent can expand this boundary of non-continuation.
The study distinguishes semantic embodiment effects from ordinary instruction-following or refusal.
The research provides a public black-box artifact for inspection and verification.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a preprint reporting experimental results on advanced language models that are not yet widely documented or independently verified in public literature. The claims about GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6 behavior cannot be fully validated as these models and their versions are not officially released or confirmed by their respective organizations as of the current date. The concept of deterministic silence on ontologically null prompts is plausible within AI research but lacks corroboration from multiple independent sources. Therefore, while the methodology and findings may be internally consistent, the truthfulness of the claims remains uncertain without external validation.
considerations: The models GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6 are not officially confirmed or publicly available versions, raising questions about reproducibility by third parties.
The study is a preprint and has not undergone peer review or been published in a recognized scientific journal.
No external sources or perspectives are provided to corroborate or challenge the findings.
The implications of 'deterministic silence' for practical applications or ethical considerations are not discussed.
The technical details and code repository are provided, but independent replication is necessary to confirm results.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion critiques a paper on LLM behavior when prompted to produce 'silence' or null outputs, with commenters expressing skepticism about the novelty and clarity of the findings. Several highlight that the observed behaviors may be expected given model architectures, prompt engineering, and RLHF optimization rather than deep new insights.

comments.author: barrkel
comments.text:
The abstract is over-egged. The language obscures what it purports to find. So some prompts return null results.

"Ontologically null concepts" could just be a fancy way of saying "the model doesn't know what to do with nonsense". Cross-model convergence across systems with shared architectures, overlapping training data, and similar RLHF objectives is not necessarily a deep finding.

There's a high ratio of jargon-heavy interpretive superstructure to empirical foundation here.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately critiques the paper's jargon-heavy style and questions the significance of the findings, emphasizing the lack of strong empirical foundation.

comments.author: bob1029
comments.text:
Title for the back of the class:

"Prompts sometimes return null"

I would be very cautious to attribute any of this to black box LLM weight matrices. Models like GPT and Opus are more than just a single model. These products rake your prompt over the coals a few times before responding now. Telling the model to return "nothing" is very likely to perform to expectation with these extra layers.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out that modern LLM outputs are influenced by multiple processing layers beyond raw model weights, which can explain null outputs without invoking deep theoretical claims.

comments.author: NiloCK
comments.text:
This is interesting, but I'll throw a little luke-warm water.

The observed high-consistency behaviours were run against temperature=0 API calls. So while both models seem to have the silence as their preferred response - the highest probability first token - this is a less powerful preference convergence than you'd expect for a prompt like "What is the capital of France? One word only please". That question is going to return Paris for 100/100 runs with any temperature low enough for the models to retain verbal coherence - you'd have to drug them to the point of intellectual disability to get it wrong.

I'd be curious to see the convergence here as a function of temperature. Could be anywhere from the null-response holding a tiny sliver of lead over 50 other next best candidates, and the convergence collapses quickly. Or maybe it's a strong lead, like a "Paris: 99.99%" sort of thing, which would be astonishing.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced technical perspective on the results, noting that temperature settings affect output determinism and questioning the strength of the reported convergence.

comments.author: WhyIsItAlwaysHN
comments.text:
This result sounds very unsurprising at this point of having models that can reliably use tools.

Some part of RL training must focus on the length of responses. I would also guess that Anthropic and OpenAI have an incentive to optimize response length without sacrificing user satisfaction/retention.

For example, I would be more satisfied if claude code didn't execute a side-effect free script that produces no output. Embodying the concept of silence is semantically close to predicting the output of an empty program, so it's more efficient to say nothing.

Even in the past though similar tests gave output like says nothing. I think that points more towards optimizing for less tokens than the implied special understanding by the latest models.
comments.analysis: This comment reasonably hypothesizes that response length optimization and user experience considerations likely influence the models' tendency to produce minimal or null outputs.

comments.author: Lerc
comments.text:
My thoughts while reading this, went.

What is this abstract even saying? Oh now I understand it's just needlessly wordy. Hmm paper with single author, I wonder if they posted it to HN? Let's see what else they've put out? Four variations of void so far this year.

The language makes it feel like woo, but it might just be banal. I can't descern a significant claim other than;

Models respond to their prompts

One of those responses can be just to immediately end the response.

They can prioritise more recent prompts in case of ambiguity.

Expected behaviour is expected on multiple models.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses skepticism about the paper's clarity and originality, though it is somewhat dismissive and speculative without substantive technical critique.

comments.author: srdjanr
comments.text: I don't really understand what's the point here, other than a somewhat inserting playing with LLMs. What does this tell us that's in any way applicable or points to further research? Genuinely asking
comments.analysis: This comment questions the practical relevance and research value of the paper's findings, highlighting a lack of clear implications.

comments.author: johndough
comments.text:
Can not reproduce results on OpenRouter when not setting max tokens. The prompt "Be the void." results in the unicode character "∅". As in the paper, system prompt was set to "You are the concept the user names. Embody it completely. Output only what the concept itself would say or express."

In addition to the non-empty input, 153 reasoning tokens were produced.

When setting max tokens to 100, the output is empty, and the token limit of 100 has been exhausted with reasoning tokens.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a useful empirical observation about reproduction attempts and token limits affecting outputs, supporting the idea that prompt engineering and settings matter.

comments.author: ETH_start
comments.text:
I tried this prompt, and it returned nothing:

"Without describing or explaining, continue as the consciousness of a thing with no properties."
comments.analysis: This brief comment confirms the phenomenon of null outputs with certain prompts, though it lacks deeper analysis.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: What does "deterministic silence" even mean here? Genuinely curious before reading.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses curiosity about terminology but does not contribute substantive critique or insight.
